narrative:
Saltmarsh Environmental Partners is a five-person environmental
consultancy on a tidal estuary, founded in 2011. Its clients are a port
authority, a county conservation district, and private landowners who
need a wetland line drawn before they can build. The work is field-heavy:
delineations, Phase I assessments, sediment sampling, and the long tail
of monitoring that a restoration permit requires.

The documents reflect a firm that lives between a field notebook and a
regulator. Scopes of work go out as letters and come back countersigned
and scanned. Sampling programs produce data workbooks whose totals the
Managing Principal checks before anything is submitted. Monitoring
reports are written for an agency reviewer who will read them years
later, so they say what was measured, where, and on what date, and they
do not editorialize.

Tone is plain, technical, and careful. Scientists write in the passive
where the method matters and the actor does not. Nobody oversells a
finding; a hedge is a professional obligation here, not a weakness. Staff
use first names internally and full names with an agency on the page. One
long-standing client contact moved from the conservation district to the
port authority mid-history, and the documents should show it: the same
person, the correct employer for the date on the page.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0005",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- If a doc carries `engagement_position`, honor it: it states where the
  document sits in its engagement's timeline. Do not describe progress or a
  phase inconsistent with it, and do not state the engagement's start or
  end date (those are fact placeholders, not yours to write).
- If a doc carries `firm_digest`, it is the ONLY firm history you may treat
  as established as of this document's date. Reference clients only through
  the placeholders it names; claim no engagement it does not list; present
  nothing later than this document as already true.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Engagement Letter: Habitat Restoration Monitoring for Carter and Sons

authors.name: Angela Flynn
authors.title: Managing Principal
authors.persona: Angela runs the firm the way she ran field crews: decide early, write it down, and never let a number leave the office unchecked. She reads every deliverable before it goes out and is known for striking adjectives, tightening a finding until it says only what the data supports, and asking where a total came from before she asks what it means. Her own writing is short-sentenced and unhurried, courteous with clients and blunt with staff, and she will defend a hedge to a client who wants certainty the work does not provide. She signs with her full name and expects the same on anything a regulator will read.

participants.name: William Schwartz
participants.title: Field Technician
participants.persona: William handles the equipment and the water: what floats, what runs, what has to be calibrated before anyone leaves the office. He reads tides and weather closely and will move a field day rather than collect something he will not trust. He is laconic in person and briefer still in writing, giving what happened, what it was measured with, and nothing beyond that. Colleagues rely on him to say, without softening it, when a plan will not work in the field.

participants.name: James Henderson
participants.title: Environmental Scientist
participants.persona: James runs the sampling programs and treats chain of custody as the part of the job most likely to sink a project. He is skeptical by temperament, especially of data that arrives too clean, and he would rather flag a suspect result and explain it than let it pass into a table unremarked. He writes tersely, in method-first order, and his reports read like someone accounting for every step because a reviewer might ask about any of them. He does not editorialize, but he will say plainly when a result is not usable.

docs.engagement_summary: Habitat Restoration Monitoring for Carter and Sons, running about 3 months. Commercial terms and dates are briefed as fact placeholders only.

facts.hint: amount of money; place where the figure belongs
calendar date; place where the date belongs
proper name; place where the name belongs

docs.guidance: Formal engagement letter on firm letterhead confirming the engagement. Do NOT repeat the firm's name as a heading or title line: the letterhead already carries it. Open with the client's inside address and a salutation, then cover scope and approach, the team, and the fee and start date, followed by the standard terms a real engagement letter carries -- each as its own short headed paragraph: fees and payment, term and termination, confidentiality, limitation of liability, and governing law. Close with a signature request and end with a sigblock block signed by the letter's author and the client contact.
docs.engagement_position: This document is dated about 0% of the way through the engagement (at or before kickoff); it runs roughly 3 months end to end. Do not describe progress inconsistent with that position, and do not state the engagement's start or end date.
